EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large boulder slid down a hillside onto Highway 2 just west of Leavenworth causing extensive damage to the roadway.  The boulder was 25 feet long and 20 feet wide, and created a hole in the highway three to five feet deep, and cracks 75 to 100 feet long.

Rain and frozen ground let to rapid runoff along hillsides and creating flooding at the intersection of Highway 20 and Pleasant Valley Road.  The upper end of Lower Pleasant Valley Road was also partially washed out.  One residence had a thick layer of rock and soil in the yard with minimal damage to the home.  The flood occurred in the Oden Road Fire area which burned during the summer of 2009.
A strong cold front passage brought estimated wind gusts of 50 miles per hour in Cheney.  The winds resulted in power outages for a couple hours, with several trees knocked down.
A southeast wind event occurred over several northern zones on the morning of November 17.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Guemes Island DW2278 had sustained 40 mph at 752 AM.

A moist southerly flow on December 28 was followed by a strong cold front on December 29. Over a 48 hour period, the Blue Mountains in southeast Washington above 4000 feet received over 2 feet of snow. Reported snowfall amounts in inches included 44 inches at Ski Bluewood.
Heavy rain resulted in water washing out the shoulder of several roads in Pearrygin Lake State Park Campgrounds.  This caused gravel to cover roads in several locations.
Winds of 32 mph and gusts to 50 mph fanned a wildfire just east of Dallesport which burned several hundred acres.  The fire started near the intersection of Highways 14 and 197.

An intense low pressure system to the north brought very tight pressure gradients resulting in high winds across the Kittitas Valley.  Gusts to 58 mph were reported at the Ellensburg Airport and sustained winds to 42 mph were reported at the Rocky Canyon DOT weather site.  A sign was blown off a roof onto a travel trailer. Kittcomm reported downed trees and power outages.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds reported in Ocean Park on the Long Beach Peninsula.  Sustained winds of 43 kt with gusts to 56 kt were reported at Cape Disappointment.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong front moved over the coast from off the Pacific, bringing strong winds to the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong peak wind gust was reported at Cape Disappointment.  Other high wind reports include: sustained winds to 44 kt with gusts to 53 kt at Toke Point; peak wind gusts to 60 kt and 52 kt by a couple trained spotters in Ocean Park on the Long Beach Peninsula.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist late season storm brought heavy snow to the Cascades.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 12 and 25 inches of snow fell at various SNOTEL sites across the area, many of which were located on Mt. St. Helens.  Stations include: Spirit Lake, June Lake, Sheep Canyon, Pigtail Peak, Surprise Lakes, and Lone Pine.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was spotted eight miles north-northwest of Creston.

A line of showers and thunderstorms in the Dusty area resulted in local damage.  More than a foot of mud washed into a barn and yard after 1.4 inches of rain fell.  In addition, lightning damaged a computer and knocked out phone and on-line services for some homes.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The peak wind gust was recorded at Cape Disappointment, along with sustained winds of 47 kt.

CZ_NAME: WEST SLOPES CENTRAL CASCADES AND PASSES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A skier was injured when an avalanche he created slammed him into a tree.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A skier was injured when an avalanche he created slammed him into a tree.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A well defined funnel cloud was reported by a number of local sources with photos and video. Survey team visited the area, talked with witnesses and despite the impressive photos and videos, the team found no evidence the funnel touched down near the honor farm just south of the Monroe correctional facility. The funnel was visible for close to 10 minutes. A second almost horizontal funnel cloud also appeared as the initial one dissipated, but lasted for only about a minute.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A well defined funnel cloud was reported by a number of local sources with photos and video. Survey team visited the area, talked with witnesses and despite the impressive photos and videos, the team found no evidence the funnel touched down near the honor farm just south of the Monroe correctional facility. The funnel was visible for close to 10 minutes. A second almost horizontal funnel cloud also appeared as the initial one dissipated, but lasted for only about a minute.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Spanish Castle Fire, centered three miles west of Trinidad, WA, was a human caused fire that burned 2,470 acres.
The Line Fire, centered four miles southeast of Wellpinit, WA, was a fire of unknown cause that burned 920 acres.
Afternoon thunderstorms produced isolated damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck an 80 foot fir tree and started a house fire.  The lightning split the tree and the energy was transferred into the cast iron sewer pipe and into the home catching the house on fire.  A large piece of the split tree hit a car that was parked across the street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm resulted in large hail seven miles southeast of Asotin.  Hail up to 1.5 inches in diameter was reported from this storm.
A squall line produced outflow winds with gusts between 45 and 70 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: About 1500 homes in the Snohomish county area lost power.  Several trees fell on homes, one man was pinned down by debris after a tree came down on his home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm resulted in large hail seven miles southeast of Asotin.  Hail up to 1.5 inches in diameter was reported from this storm.
The Buffalo Lake Fire, centered five miles northeast of Coulee Dam, was a human caused fire that burned 408 acres.
The Doneen Rainey Fire, located near Badger Mountain, burned nearly 2,000 acres of grass, brush, and wheat.  The fire came close to homes in the Canyon Hills subdivision above Fancher Heights.

A thunderstorm containing very heavy rainfall tracked through Ephrata resulting in Urban Flooding.  Storm drains were overwhelmed by the heavy rain with a road bed washed out.  Water from a sewer backed up towards a house.  Water in the city streets got as high as 18 to 20 inches deep.  The Ephrata Airport reported 0.29 inches of rain in 25 minutes.  Other rain gauges just west of Ephrata received around a half inch of rain.
The Baird Springs Fire, centered five miles northwest of Quincy, was a fire of unknown cause that burned 7,693 acres.
A strong Fall storm brought the first heavy snow accumulations to the south Washington Cascades.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall estimates were reported to be 21 to 22 inches over the area, based on reports from the Pigtail Peak, Sheep Canyon and Surprise Lakes SNOTEL sites.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Baird Springs Fire, centered five miles northwest of Quincy, was a fire of unknown cause that burned 7,693 acres.

CZ_NAME: WEST SLOPES NORTHERN CASCADES AND PASSES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wet Pacific frontal system produced heavy snow amounts at Mt Baker on Jan 1.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The NW Weather and Avalanche Center precipitation gage at Mt Baker recorded 12 inches of snowfall during 12 hours ending at 11 pm Jan 1.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous low pressure system off the coast generated high winds to the Olympic Mountains, particularly to areas exposed to southerly winds.  The high winds lasted for 10 hours.  Brief local high winds also occurred along the neighboring north coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusted well over 100 mph on top of Hurricane Ridge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous low pressure system off the coast generated high winds to the Olympic Mountains, particularly to areas exposed to southerly winds.  The high winds lasted for 10 hours.  Brief local high winds also occurred along the neighboring north coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Destruction Island recorded 53 KT sustained wind that translates to a 61 mph gust over the land.  KUIL - Quillayute Prairie Airport near Forks also recorded a peak gust of 60 mph at 559 PM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Brief east high wind was reported in Enumclaw.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 758 AM Sat Dec 4, a NWS weather spotter in Enumclaw reported 40 mph winds with gusts up to 66 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This was a classic east wind event in the east Puget Sound lowlands with some evidence of flow reversal and mountain waves forming.  The surface gradient was also quite strong--strong enough to cause high wind at Tatoosh Island (TTI).  There was widespread damage in the Enumclaw and North Bend areas, and some damage farther west in Federal Way.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusted to 70 mph in Enumclaw.  Just over 110,000 customers lost power.  Downed power lines caused a house to burn to the ground in Enumclaw and in the same area, a barn was destroyed and parts of the roof blew off a PSE building.  Several roads were closed due to fallen trees and power poles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This was a classic east wind event in the east Puget Sound lowlands with some evidence of flow reversal and mountain waves forming.  The surface gradient was also quite strong--strong enough to cause high wind at Tatoosh Island (TTI).  There was widespread damage in the Enumclaw and North Bend areas, and some damage farther west in Federal Way.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: From 12/17 1130 PM to 12/18 310 AM, Tatoosh Island (TTI) had 58+ mph sustained wind.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line produced outflow winds with gusts between 45 and 70 mph.
A lightning storm knocked two Forks radio stations off the air and damaged some equipment.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning storm knocked two Forks radio stations off the air and damaged some equipment.  It also caused a fire that destroyed a nearby home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High winds were recorded in several zones, mainly on the coast and over the northwest interior, but extending south into the Everett zone and also hitting the Lake Lawrence area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tatoosh Island and Destruction Island C-MAN weather stations both recorded sustained wind over 50 KT that translates to gusts over 58 mph onshore.  KUIL - Quillayute Prairie Airport near Forks also had a 61 mph gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific cold front moved through the Cascades bringing heavy precipitation and dramatically lowering snow levels.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sheep Canyon reported 18 inches of new snow.  White Pass, WA also reported 20 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold pool of air aloft produced a very unstable airmass over southwest Washington. A vigorous low pressure center was just off the Washington Coast with a surface trough moving through southwest Washington. This trough served as a focus for thunderstorms during the day. These thunderstorms produced strong, gusty winds in several locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gusty winds were reported at Long Beach, Washington with a peak gust of 55 kts.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was major flooding along the Snoqualmie River.  Westside Hwy on Vashon island was closed due to a portion of the road sinking.  Some basements flooded as China Creek in Newcastle, overflowed.  Several roads around North Bend and Carnation were closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low approaching the coast from the southwest brought strong southerly winds to the Pacific Northwest...especially along the south Washington coast and interior portions of southwest Washington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Coast Guard Station at Cape Disappointment recorded sustained winds of 41 kts with gusts to 53 kts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Stillaguamish River reached record level.  There were several roads washed out in Kitsap county.  2 homes were damaged from mudslides.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: About 33 homes, and one business, were damaged in Granite Falls, Arlington, and Stanwood when the Stillaguamish River hit record flood stage.  Parts of Highway 530 were closed.  About 240 homes were issued a voluntary evacuation, while 6 people had to be rescued by hovercraft near Granite Falls.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Stillaguamish River reached record level.  There were several roads washed out in Kitsap county.  2 homes were damaged from mudslides.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: All of highway 106 in Mason county was closed due to flooding and mudslides.  A foot bridge in the Tahuya State Forest was closed after floods washed out the concrete entry.  Several homes were reported flooded in Mason county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist warm front passage resulted in heavy snow over the northeast Washington Mountains and the Blue Mountains.   In the Northeast Washington Mountains, Clayton picked up four inches of snow.  In the Blue Mountains, snow accumulations ranged from seven to 13 inches.

A very moist warm front passage resulted in heavy snow over the northeast Washington Mountains and the Blue Mountains.   In the Northeast Washington Mountains, Clayton picked up four inches of snow.  In the Blue Mountains, snow accumulations ranged from seven to 13 inches.
The Stillaguamish River reached record level.  There were several roads washed out in Kitsap county.  2 homes were damaged from mudslides.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was some damage along the Skagit river.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High winds were recorded in several zones, mainly on the coast and over the northwest interior, but extending south into the Everett zone and also hitting the Lake Lawrence area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NOS site at Cherry Point recorded sustained 44 mph wind gusting to 59 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rain on frozen ground triggered a small mudslide which went across the Loomis Highway near Whitestone Lake.  The mudslide blocked westbound traffic which forced a brief road closure.
During an easterly wind event, one site reported high wind.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter near Sandy Point reported an east wind of 42g55 mph at 905 AM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a more widespread event elsewhere in western Washington earlier in the day, high wind occurred in the east Puget Sound lowlands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter near Enumclaw reported a sustained eas wind of 50 mph gusting to 80 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell over the Spokane area and Northeast Washington Mountains.  A period of moderate to locally heavy accumulations occurred on the 25th as a warm front passed through.  Then after a break early on the 26th, snow increased again and became heavy at times as a cold front slowly moved through the area.  Snow accumulations over the three day period ranged from six to ten inches, with locally higher amounts.  The National Weather Service office near Airway Heights recorded 11 inches of snow.

A band of heavy snow accumulations occurred from the Moses Lake area extending into Northeast Washington as a deep low pressure system impacted the area.  Most locations picked up between four to eight inches of snow.  On the Palouse, State Highways 23, 194, and 272 were closed due to blowing and drifting snow.

A band of heavy snow accumulations occurred from the Moses Lake area extending into Northeast Washington as a deep low pressure system impacted the area.  Most locations picked up between four to eight inches of snow.  On the Palouse, State Highways 23, 194, and 272 were closed due to blowing and drifting snow.

A deep low pressure system tracking through Oregon in combination with an arctic area of high pressure over British Columbia resulted in strong northerly winds and snow with areas of blizzard conditions.  Washington state activated its Emergency Operations Center. There were numerous school closures and traffic accidents.  Portions of State Routes 2, 21, 23, 27, 28, 195, 231, and 261 were closed due to heavy snow, blowing snow, and drifting snow. In Grant County, Washington State Patrol responded to about four dozen snow related incidents, including vehicles sliding off roadways or becoming stuck in drifts.  At least 13 vehicles were reported sliding off Interstate 90 in the Sprague area.  In Wenatchee police responded to 34 traffic accidents due to slick roads.  One mile west of Wenatchee, two people were injured when losing control on the snow and ice.  In Chelan, a large locust tree was blown over and damaged a fence.  Snow accumulations from the storm ranged from four to eight inches, with locally nine inches.  The highest accumulations reported of nine inches were seven miles north of Newport, and in Colfax.

A strong cold front brought high winds to portions of Central and Eastern Washington.  In addition, as the front approached the Spokane area extending south to the Palouse and Blue Mountains, thunderstorms developed resulting in even stronger wind gusts.  Numerous power outages and down trees were reported during the storm.  The trees damaged several cars and homes.  A portion of the High School roof in Clarkston was blown off.   A communications tower atop the Asotin County Jail was blown over.  In Pullman, shingles were blown off of homes.  One particular tree in Pullman landed on a portion of a home, destroying a collectable car, boat, travel trailer, and golf cart.  Estimated damage from this report was $40,000.  In Albion shingles were blown off the post office roof.  In East Wenatchee the winds blew a carport 300 feet.  The lightning was isolated so the event was entered as both a thunderstorm wind and high wind event.  The exception to this was over the Palouse where the lightning was abundant.  However winds prior to the front gusted to 60 miles per hour in Pullman.  Wind gusts reported during the storm in miles per hour include: 85 in Pullman, 64 Wenatchee, 63 Uniontown, 59 Ephrata, and 58 in Beverly.

CZ_NAME: EASTERN STRAIT OF JUAN DE FUCA
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: South winds of 20 to 30 mph and gusts to 45 mph occurred on the evening of November 15 in parts of western Washington and then after the cold front passed, strong onshore flow brought marginal high wind to a few zones, mainly near the Strait of Juan de Fuca.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An observation southeast of Sequim at 1476 ft recorded gusts of 65+ mph 825 PM to 845 PM.  About 25,000 people lost power in the greater Port Angeles area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: South winds of 20 to 30 mph and gusts to 45 mph occurred on the evening of November 15 in parts of western Washington and then after the cold front passed, strong onshore flow brought marginal high wind to a few zones, mainly near the Strait of Juan de Fuca.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter in Enumclaw reported a 60 mph gust at 940 PM.  There were about 10,000 customers who lost power along the foothills.  Around Granite Falls and Darrington, several trees fell, 4 of them hitting houses or cars.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts blew down trees and branches in the Tacoma area.  The strong wind damaged and tore loose a boathouse and knocked out power to about 55,000 people.

A cold easterly wind through the Columbia River Gorge was keeping cold air trapped in the Gorge as a strong Pacific frontal system moved inland. This system spread precipitation over the Gorge starting as snow and changing over to freezing rain as the air mass warmed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter, SA-26, from Carson, Washington reported 7 inches of new snow accumulation between 20:00 PST on November 29th and 08:00 PST on November 30th. Another spotter, SA-10, from Stevenson, Washington reported 1/4 inch ice accumulation between 04:00 PST and 09:00 PST on November 30th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm brought damaging winds during the early morning to portions of Walla Walla.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm with winds gusting to 58 mph knocked down trees and caused power outages to over 3500 customers in Walla Walla.  One large tree fell onto an unoccupied car and caused major damage.

A strong low pressure system dropped south out of British Columbia bringing cold air and heavy snow to the south Washington Cascades and foothills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Calamity SNOTEL site measured 13 inches of new snow between 07:00 PST and 18:00 PST on November 22nd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An abnormally cold air mass was in place over southwest Washington  with an easterly wind blowing through the Columbia River Gorge. The precipitation from an incoming Pacific frontal system fell through this air mass as freezing rain in the Gorge causing travel to be treacherous.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter, Stevenson SA-10, reported 1/2 inch accumulation of ice at 09:00 PST on November 26th. The Department of Transportation closed Interstate 84 from Cascade Locks, Oregon to Hood River, Oregon for dangerous icy condition on the roadway. Given the conditions of the storm, it is safe to assume the same dangerous conditions were present on State Highway 14 in Washington.

An arctic air mass, fresh snow cover, and clear skies resulted in extremely cold temperatures on the morning of November 24th in the Columbia basin and portions of the immediate surrounding area.  Low temperatures reported in degrees Fahrenheit include -25 in Davenport, -24 at Turnbull RAWS in Cheney, -22 in La Crosse, and -19 in Moses Lake.  Ephrata reported a low -14 but a 5 to 10 MPH wind resulted in a wind chill factor of -27 degrees Fahrenheit.  Due to the extremely cold temperatures, the main electric breakers became overloaded at the Moses Lake substation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: South winds of 20 to 30 mph and gusts to 45 mph occurred on the evening of November 15 in parts of western Washington and then after the cold front passed, strong onshore flow brought marginal high wind to a few zones, mainly near the Strait of Juan de Fuca.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Whidbey Island Naval Air Station (KNUW) recorded a peak gust of 61 mph.  One school was closed the next morning due to a power outage.  Several roads were blocked by downed trees.  A hangar's roof at the Naval Air Station sustained minor damage.  Two ferry runs were canceled.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep low pressure system tracking through Oregon in combination with an arctic area of high pressure over British Columbia resulted in strong northerly winds and snow with areas of blizzard conditions.  Washington state activated its Emergency Operations Center. There were numerous school closures and traffic accidents.  Portions of State Routes 2, 21, 23, 27, 28, 195, 231, and 261 were closed due to heavy snow, blowing snow, and drifting snow. In Grant County, Washington State Patrol responded to about four dozen snow related incidents, including vehicles sliding off roadways or becoming stuck in drifts.  At least 13 vehicles were reported sliding off Interstate 90 in the Sprague area.  In Wenatchee police responded to 34 traffic accidents due to slick roads.  One mile west of Wenatchee, two people were injured when losing control on the snow and ice.  In Chelan, a large locust tree was blown over and damaged a fence.  Snow accumulations from the storm ranged from four to eight inches, with locally nine inches.  The highest accumulations reported of nine inches were seven miles north of Newport, and in Colfax.

A strong cold front brought high winds to portions of Central and Eastern Washington.  In addition, as the front approached the Spokane area extending south to the Palouse and Blue Mountains, thunderstorms developed resulting in even stronger wind gusts.  Numerous power outages and down trees were reported during the storm.  The trees damaged several cars and homes.  A portion of the High School roof in Clarkston was blown off.   A communications tower atop the Asotin County Jail was blown over.  In Pullman, shingles were blown off of homes.  One particular tree in Pullman landed on a portion of a home, destroying a collectable car, boat, travel trailer, and golf cart.  Estimated damage from this report was $40,000.  In Albion shingles were blown off the post office roof.  In East Wenatchee the winds blew a carport 300 feet.  The lightning was isolated so the event was entered as both a thunderstorm wind and high wind event.  The exception to this was over the Palouse where the lightning was abundant.  However winds prior to the front gusted to 60 miles per hour in Pullman.  Wind gusts reported during the storm in miles per hour include: 85 in Pullman, 64 Wenatchee, 63 Uniontown, 59 Ephrata, and 58 in Beverly.

An arctic air mass, fresh snow cover, and clear skies resulted in extremely cold temperatures on the morning of November 24th in the Columbia basin and portions of the immediate surrounding area.  Low temperatures reported in degrees Fahrenheit include -25 in Davenport, -24 at Turnbull RAWS in Cheney, -22 in La Crosse, and -19 in Moses Lake.  Ephrata reported a low -14 but a 5 to 10 MPH wind resulted in a wind chill factor of -27 degrees Fahrenheit.  Due to the extremely cold temperatures, the main electric breakers became overloaded at the Moses Lake substation.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snoqualmie Pass, a Northwest Weather and Avalanche Center observation site, reported wind of 53g93 mph at 10 PM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A southeast wind event occurred over several northern zones on the morning of November 17.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Whidbey Island Naval Air Station (KNUW) had 44g60 mph wind 643 AM to 756 AM, Port Townsend C8316 had sustained 40+ mph wind 839 AM to 859 AM, Keystone ferry terminal had sustained 40+ mph wind 655 AM to 915 AM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A southeast wind event occurred over several northern zones on the morning of November 17.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Bellingham area citizen weather observation program site CW9778 had sustained 40+ mph wind 823 AM to 939 AM.

CZ_NAME: WEST SLOPES CENTRAL CASCADES AND PASSES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific frontal system and the associated westerly flow behind it, brought heavy snow to the Cascades as well as local freezing rain in the major passes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The NW Weather and Avalanche Center (NWAC) station at Alpental recorded 18 inches snow between 4 pm Nov 29 and 2 pm Nov 30. The NWAC station at Paradise recorded 18 inches snow between 5 pm Nov 29 and 1 pm Nov 30.

Heavy snow fell over the Spokane area and Northeast Washington Mountains.  A period of moderate to locally heavy accumulations occurred on the 25th as a warm front passed through.  Then after a break early on the 26th, snow increased again and became heavy at times as a cold front slowly moved through the area.  Snow accumulations over the three day period ranged from six to ten inches, with locally higher amounts.  The National Weather Service office near Airway Heights recorded 11 inches of snow.
A series of strong weather systems brought heavy snow accumulations to the south Washington Cascades.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Lone Pine SNOTEL site measured 16 inches of new snow. Other reports of heavy snow were 13 inches at the Spencer Meadow SNOTEL.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A relatively strong surface low moved into British Columbia on the morning of the 15th, draping a cold front just off the northwest Oregon coast. This front caused strong winds to occur along the north and central Oregon coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Coast Guard Station at Cape Disappointment coast recorded sustained winds of 47mph, with wind gusts up to 71mph.  Additionally, a trained spotter in Ocean Park measured a peak gust of 58mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of lows moved through the northeast Pacific on the 17th and 18th, giving high winds to the south Washington coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Coast Guard Station at Cape Disappointment recorded sustained wind speeds of 59 mph, with a maximum gust to 72mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High winds were recorded in several zones, mainly on the coast and over the northwest interior, but extending south into the Everett zone and also hitting the Lake Lawrence area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: KPAE - Paine Field had a 60 mph gust.  A NWS employee near Mill Creek estimated gusts up to 60 mph.  About 9000 customers lost power in Snohomish County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong warm front passage resulted in heavy snow accumulations in the Central Washington valleys as well as the Northeast Washington Mountains.   Snow accumulations in the Wenatchee area, Waterville Plateau, Okanogan Valley, and Okanogan Highlands ranged from four to six inches.  In the Northeast Washington Mountains, snow levels were generally above 3000 feet but a few valley locations received snowfall.  Ione received five inches of snow, while Chewelah and Springdale received four inches.  In the mountains, 49 North Ski Resort picked up eight inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quick moving low pressure system moved through the northeast Pacific on January 11, 2010.  The associated cold front caused some brief high winds along the south Washington coast during the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Coast Guard Station at Cape Disappointment Coast recorded sustained winds of 45 mph, with gusts up to 54 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: KHQM - Hoquiam's Bowerman Field, reported a gust of 62 mph.  About 1500 residents of Grays Harbor County lost power.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High winds were recorded in several zones, mainly on the coast and over the northwest interior, but extending south into the Everett zone and also hitting the Lake Lawrence area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesonet weather reporting site 5 miles north of Oak Harbor recorded sustained wind of 43 mph gusting to 58 mph.  A few trees were downed and power was knocked out to about 2400 customers on Whidbey Island.  Two morning ferry runs were canceled.  Part of a metal roof on a Marina dock was ripped off by the wind.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High winds were recorded in several zones, mainly on the coast and over the northwest interior, but extending south into the Everett zone and also hitting the Lake Lawrence area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter near Lake Lawrence reported 44 mph sustained wind with 60 mph gusts. Several trees were downed and about 4000 customers lost power in Lewis County, one tree fell on a garage.  Fallen trees damaged a carport in Tumwater, and nearly 30,000 customers lost power in Thurston County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell over portions of central and northeast Washington.  The Waterville Plateau was hit hard where 18 to 24 inches of snow fell.  Daily snowfall records were broken for several areas including Mazama with 21 inches, and Holden Village with 17 inches.  Power went out twice in East Wenatchee affecting over 800 customers.  Brief power outages were also reported in Chelan, Entiat, and Mansfield.

Daily snowfall records were broken for several areas including Mazama with 21 inches, and Holden Village with 17 inches.  Power went out twice in East Wenatchee affecting over 800 customers.  Brief power outages were also reported in Chelan, Entiat, and Mansfield.

Heavy snow fell over much of Eastern Washington, as well as blizzard conditions on the Palouse.  Widespread accidents, major highway closures, and thousands losing power resulted during this major storm. Road closures include U.S. Highway 195 from Pullman to the Idaho State Line, State Route 27 from Palouse to Garfield, and State Route 23 from Steptoe to the Lincoln County Line. In addition, Pullman-Moscow Regional Airport closed early on the morning of December 29th due to poor runway conditions, and remained closed much of the day.  Washington State Patrol responded to 150 slide offs and collisions between Cheney and the Idaho State Line.  Interstate 90 in Spokane was closed at the height of the storm after a semi truck crashed.  Two people received indirect injuries after a collision on Highway 195.  County roads from Wilson Creek to Grand Coulee were closed due to falling and drifting snow.

A strong low approaching the coast from the southwest brought strong southerly winds to the Pacific Northwest...especially along the south Washington coast and interior portions of southwest Washington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds were reported at the Larch Mountain RAWS site with a gust to 57 kts. A trained spotter in Vancouver reported three-inch branches snapping off trees. Strong winds gusting to 47 kts in Battle Ground caused broken tree branches and power outages. The Emergency Manager for Clark County reported 8500 people without power overnight due to large downed trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An occluded front followed by a slow moving cold front passage brought heavy snow to the Northeast Washington Mountains.  Mount Spokane Ski Resort picked up 13 inches of new snow, with six to 14 inches at 49 Degrees Ski Resort.

A cold and unstable air mass in combination with low level upslope flow brought heavy snow on the Waterville Plateau.  A trained spotter reported six inches of snow in Waterville.  Nine miles north of Chelan on Highway 97, a driver lost control on the ice resulting in a collision with one indirect fatality and at least two indirect injuries.

Nine miles north of Chelan on Highway 97, a driver lost control on the ice resulting in a collision with one indirect fatality and at least two indirect injuries.
A fast moving cold front brought heavy snow to the south Washington Cascades.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Pigtail Peak SNOTEL site measured 20 inches of new snow between 06:00 PST on  November 17th and 07:00 PST on November 18th.  Eighteen inches of new snow was reported at Sheep Canyon SNOTEL and at Swift Creek SNOTEL between 08:00 PST on November 17th and 04:00 PST on November 18th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front brought a brief period of high winds to the south Washington coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The national weather service equipment at the Cape Disappointment coast guard station measured sustained winds of 39 knots, with gusts to 50 knots.

CZ_NAME: EAST SLOPES OF THE SOUTHERN CASCADES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tight pressure gradient combined with a cold frontal passage to produce widespread high winds and damage. Blowing dust reduced visibility to near zero in portions of the Yakima Valley and Lower Columbia Basin and forced road closures. Peak wind gusts in mph included 15 miles west of Basin City (77), 8 miles northwest of Richland (59), 1 mile northwest of Richland (58), Benton City (58), 5 miles south of Eltopia (58), 1 miles north northeast of College Place (58), 8 miles northwest of West Richland (74), and High Bridge RAWS (59). Sustained winds at least 40 mph were reported 8 miles north of Dayton and 2 miles west northwest of College Place.  Downed trees, branches, power lines and property damage were reported at West Richland, 7 miles southeast of White Swan, 5 miles north northwest of Grandview, Benton City, Dayton, 1 mile north northeast of College Place, Prosser, 8 miles southeast of Dayton, Walla Walla, 1 mile northeast of Sunnyside, and Bickleton.

Strong winds associated with a cold frontal passage blew through the Hanford Reservation.   Peak wind gusts in miles per hour included 8 miles northwest of Richland (60) and 14 miles north northwest of Richland (58).
A fast moving cold front and strong winds aloft produced severe thunderstorms with damaging outflow winds into College Place.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and powers lines were knocked down on Highway 125 due to thunderstorm winds.  The College Place Airport mesonet station recorded a wind gust to 50 knots.

CZ_NAME: WEST SLOPES CENTRAL CASCADES AND PASSES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Brief high wind in the Admiralty Inlet area.  Heavy snow in the central Cascades.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In the 12 hour period ending at 4 pm, both Alpental and Snoqualmie Pass got 12-15 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong frontal passage brought late season heavy snow in the higher elevations of the Okanogan Valley, Northeast Washington Mountains, and the Blue Mountains.  Snow accumulations from the Okanogan Valley include five inches seven miles northwest of Omak, and six inches near Malott.  In the northeast Washington Mountains, 49 Degrees Ski Resort reported eight inches of snow.  Over southeast Washington, Ski Bluewood reported seven inches of snow.
A deep low passed just NW of Tatoosh Island.  High wind was recorded on the coast and in a few inland zones.  Strong wind was reported in other inland zones.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: About 5000 homes lost power.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep low passed just NW of Tatoosh Island.  High wind was recorded on the coast and in a few inland zones.  Strong wind was reported in other inland zones.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tatoosh Island (TTI) had 50+ knots sustained wind for about 6 hours, including nearly 3 hours of hurricane force winds.  Destruction Island (DES) had 50+ knots on and off for about 8 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep low passed just NW of Tatoosh Island.  High wind was recorded on the coast and in a few inland zones.  Strong wind was reported in other inland zones.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hoquiam (KHQM) recorded a peak gust of 58 mph.  The Westport (WPT) tide gauge had gusts 50+ knots off and on for about 3 hours.  About 300 customers lost power near Quinault due to a downed tree branch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep low passed just NW of Tatoosh Island.  High wind was recorded on the coast and in a few inland zones.  Strong wind was reported in other inland zones.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Whidbey Island Naval Air Station (KNUW) recorded 44g62 mph at 153 PM.  Keystone ferry dock had 40 mph sustained wind at 115 PM.  A tree fell on a home in Scatchet Head, on Whidbey Island.  Several other trees fell across roads on the island.  Ferry sailings from Keystone to Port Townsend were cancelled.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cherry Point (CHY) tide gauge recorded 50+ knots gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep low passed just NW of Tatoosh Island.  High wind was recorded on the coast and in a few inland zones.  Strong wind was reported in other inland zones.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Bellingham (KBLI) recorded a 61 mph peak gust.  Sandy Pt. Shores measured 38g58 mph at 231 PM and 236 PM.  About 5,000 customers lost power.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep low passed just NW of Tatoosh Island.  High wind was recorded on the coast and in a few inland zones.  Strong wind was reported in other inland zones.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Paine Field (KPAE) measured a peak gust of 62 mph at 323 PM.  About 5000 Snohomish county customers lost power.  A few trees were downed over roads and one struck a building near Lake Stevens.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist warm front brought heavy snow to the mountains of Northeast Washington.  49 Degrees Ski Resort reported 10 inches of new snowfall.

Strong winds contributed to several power outages in the Spokane and Davenport areas due to downed power lines.  Avista Utilities reported at least 1,200 customers without power across northeast Washington.  The strongest wind gust was reported at Fairchild Air Force Base where the peak wind speed was 54 miles per hour.

A tight pressure gradient and a strong jet stream moved over the area behind a cold front resulting in a windstorm over Central and Eastern Washington.  This resulted in extensive damage, as well as a dust storm from the Moses Lake area eastward into the Upper Columbia Basin.  Numerous downed trees and power outages were reported, with some trees falling on homes.  The Clarkston and Lewiston areas were hit especially hard with wind gusts of 60-65 miles per hour resulting in 9,000 customers without power.  In Davenport, a tree fell and flattened the Old Schoolhouse and damaged a pickup.  The winds also blew shingles off some homes in the Davenport, Spokane, and Pullman areas.  On Highway 195 near Rosalia, winds contributed to a fatal motorcycle accident resulting in one indirect fatality.  In Wenatchee, strong wind gusts blew shingles off a home and damaged a restaurant sign.  On Interstate 90, exit signs near milepost 151 and milepost 221 were blown over.  Meanwhile, the high winds kicked up a significant amount of blowing dust leading to several road closures in the Moses Lake area, with visibility also reduced to less than a quarter mile in the Upper Columbia Basin in Ritzville.  Five miles west of Quincy, Highway 281 was closed in both directions due to the dust.  In Othello, Highway 17 was closed in both directions from milepost 34 to milepost 50.  Five miles east of Moses Lake, visibility near zero was reported on Wheeler Road.  Peak wind gusts in miles per hour from this event include 62 Palouse, 60 Uniontown, 60 six miles west of Clarkston at Silcott Island, 59 Spokane International Airport, 59 Moses Lake, 59 Reardan, 59 near Vantage, and 58 in Mansfield.  Sustained winds near 40 miles per hour were also measured in Spangle, 10 miles west of Coulee Dam, 12 miles west of Moses Lake, Pullman, and Fairchild Air Force Base.

CZ_NAME: EAST SLOPES OF THE CENTRAL CASCADES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Cowiche Mill Fire spread across 6200 acres of grass and brush and destroyed 1 residence and 7 outbuildings 12 miles west of Yakima.  It also damaged fences, pump houses, fruit trees and a vacant structure owned by the Department of Fish and Wildlife.   A total of 10 Strike Teams and approximately 300 personnel were deployed in support of the incident.

A gust front from a line of convective showers and embedded lightning in spots led to damage over portions of Central and Eastern Washington.  In addition, a brief tornado occurred near Moses Lake.  On the Palouse and in the Spokane area, four to six diameter tree branches were snapped off.  When the gust front hit the Ruby area, lightning was associated with it where local significant damage occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds from a gust front damaged Simplot's Moses Lake Plant, forcing it to close down for 24 hours for repairs.  The damage partially ripped the siding from the wall in the raw potato receiving area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms brought heavy rainfall to western Yakima County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall from thunderstorms caused a large volume of mud and water in Indian Creek to overwhelm a culvert and block Forest Service Road 1308 about 2 miles northwest of the junction of Highway 12.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms brought heavy rainfall to western Yakima County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRaHS observer reported 1.31 inches of rainfall with strong thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ramsey Creek Road, became closed for several weeks after heavy rain and a plugged culvert washed away 1,000 feet of road to a depth of four feet in spots.  This event occurred in early June, but the exact date and time is unknown.

The Snake River at Anatone rose to above flood stage from June 5th through June 8th leading to minor flooding.  The Snake River crested at a stage of 22.18 feet near 5 PM Pacific Standard Time on June 6th.  The river flooded a portion of Snake River Road south of Asotin, including the Heller Bar area.  One car was flooded by the river.  Large logs and debris were cleared from Snake River road.  The water was up to eight inches deep in spots.  One residence had water up to three feet deep in a barn, with damaged fences on the property.

A severe thunderstorm brought large hail, flash flooding, and high winds along Highway 2 near Hartline.  Field erosion, as well as damage to the Wheat and Barley Fields resulted from the large hail and heavy rain.  Two hundred acres of wheat was lost with estimated damage of $50,000.  The largest hailstone reported was 1.25 inches in diameter.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large hail from a thunderstorm accumulated to a depth of six inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A band of heavy rain showers led to standing water of six inches deep on Highway 20 in Winthrop.  A nearby RAWS station, NCSB, picked up 0.51 inches of rain in one hour.

Gusty winds along and behind a strong cold front passage brought high winds to the Spokane area and on the Palouse.  Several trees and limbs were blown down during the storm, leading to power outages.  About 1,000 homes were without power during the peak of the winds in the Spokane area.  In Colfax, the high winds took down a brick encased duct.

A particularly late-season frontal system brought strong winds to the coast and Willapa Hills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The peak wind gust was recorded by equipment on Mt. Hebo approximately 20 miles south of the area.  Because the anemometer was frozen during the majority of this event, this peak gust probably occurred after the peak of the winds moved through.  Start and end times were estimated. Although the instrument is in Oregon, the winds tend to be equally strong in the north as they were in the south.  Because this is a data sparse area, it can be inferred that similar winds occurred here as well.

When the gust front hit the Ruby area, lightning was associated with it where local significant damage occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF0 tornado touched down five miles northeast of Moses Lake on Northeast Road P.  Non-continuous track from south to northwest for one-half to one-third of a mile.  The tornado had an estimated width of 100 yards with winds of 65 to 85 miles per hour.  The tornado lifted a section of roof that was unattached and sitting on the ground or leaning against something on the ground. The wind lifted this unattached roof about 20 feet off the ground and dropped it 30 feet from its original location onto the old barn, destroying it.  A grain silo was damaged by the tornado.  Numerous large tree branches were downed, including one tossed 300 feet from the tree.  Numerous shingles were spread 120 yards.  The time of the tornado is estimated from 5:15 PST to 5:20 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong southerlies occurred on the night of May 2, but not high wind.  Westerlies began on the morning of May 3 and reached high wind criteria on Whidbey Island.  Pockets of strong wind occurred from Lewis to Skagit Counties.  About 15000 customers in all lost power.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: About 1900 people lost power around Anacortes.  Several trees fell, one into a power line and another blocking SR 534.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong southerlies occurred on the night of May 2, but not high wind.  Westerlies began on the morning of May 3 and reached high wind criteria on Whidbey Island.  Pockets of strong wind occurred from Lewis to Skagit Counties.  About 15000 customers in all lost power.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: About 1400 people lost power in east Jefferson county.  Several trees fell, one onto a power line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong southerlies occurred on the night of May 2, but not high wind.  Westerlies began on the morning of May 3 and reached high wind criteria on Whidbey Island.  Pockets of strong wind occurred from Lewis to Skagit Counties.  About 15000 customers in all lost power.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: About 6500 people lost power in Snohomish county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tight pressure gradient and a strong jet stream moved over the area behind a cold front resulting in a windstorm over Central and Eastern Washington.  This resulted in extensive damage, as well as a dust storm from the Moses Lake area eastward into the Upper Columbia Basin.  Numerous downed trees and power outages were reported, with some trees falling on homes.  The Clarkston and Lewiston areas were hit especially hard with wind gusts of 60-65 miles per hour resulting in 9,000 customers without power.  In Davenport, a tree fell and flattened the Old Schoolhouse and damaged a pickup.  The winds also blew shingles off some homes in the Davenport, Spokane, and Pullman areas.  On Highway 195 near Rosalia, winds contributed to a fatal motorcycle accident resulting in one indirect fatality.  In Wenatchee, strong wind gusts blew shingles off a home and damaged a restaurant sign.  On Interstate 90, exit signs near milepost 151 and milepost 221 were blown over.  Meanwhile, the high winds kicked up a significant amount of blowing dust leading to several road closures in the Moses Lake area, with visibility also reduced to less than a quarter mile in the Upper Columbia Basin in Ritzville.  Five miles west of Quincy, Highway 281 was closed in both directions due to the dust.  In Othello, Highway 17 was closed in both directions from milepost 34 to milepost 50.  Five miles east of Moses Lake, visibility near zero was reported on Wheeler Road.  Peak wind gusts in miles per hour from this event include 62 Palouse, 60 Uniontown, 60 six miles west of Clarkston at Silcott Island, 59 Spokane International Airport, 59 Moses Lake, 59 Reardan, 59 near Vantage, and 58 in Mansfield.  Sustained winds near 40 miles per hour were also measured in Spangle, 10 miles west of Coulee Dam, 12 miles west of Moses Lake, Pullman, and Fairchild Air Force Base.

An upper level low brought unstable conditions and thunderstorms with heavy rainfall, hail, and flash flooding during the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observer reported 1/2 to 3/4 inch hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low brought unstable conditions and thunderstorms with heavy rainfall, hail, and flash flooding during the afternoon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low brought unstable conditions and thunderstorms with heavy rainfall, hail, and flash flooding during the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm produced heavy rainfall, flash flooding, and pea size hail.  Hail damaged the apple crop.  Water runoff from field flowed over an irrigation canal and onto adjacent field. Drains and culverts became blocked resulting in ditches filling up, overflowing, and eroding the draing ditches.  Water flow was up to 3 feet deep in spots and flowed over the paved roads.  Rainfall of 1.25 inches was reported in 20 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong wind gust measured at 40 MPH resulted in downed power lines with power out to the whole community of Stehekin.

Temperatures topped 100 degrees for two successive days in Hanford, Pasco, Prosser, Basin City, Kennewick, Eltopia, Edna, Roosevelt, College Place, Whitman Mission, and Walla Walla.  An 86 year old Walla Walla man died of heat stroke in his home.

A severe thunderstorm brought large hail, flash flooding, and high winds along Highway 2 near Hartline.  Field erosion, as well as damage to the Wheat and Barley Fields resulted from the large hail and heavy rain.  Two hundred acres of wheat was lost with estimated damage of $50,000.  The largest hailstone reported was 1.25 inches in diameter.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very heavy rain led water running down the road and ditches along Highway Two.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms brought penny and nickel sized hail to near Waterville, Entiat, and Omak.  The strongest storm occurred on the Waterville Plateau where significant rain damage from flash flooding as well as nickel size hail fell.  Heavy rain with 2.25 inches in two hours was reported four miles northeast of Waterville which washed out a road.  The headwaters of Douglas Creek took out fences and parts of Road 11 NW.  The water was 10 feet deep in spots.  Some fields received a complete wheat loss. Meanwhile, heavy rain on the Nahahum fire north of Cashmere resulted in a shoulder being partially washed out on Nahahum Canyon Road between milepost four and five.

The Rainbow Bridge Fire, centered 1.5 miles north of Stehekin, was a fire of unknown cause which burned about 3,710 acres. The fire burned within a few hundred feet of the Stehekin School and the historic schoolhouse. A couple of homes were under mandatory evacuations. The fire started on July 29th.

A combination of gusty winds and low relative humidity contributed to a couple new large fire starts.  The Slide Creek Fire, centered six miles south of Colville, burned 989 acres, two homes, and one outbuilding.  The Fish Hatchery Fire, centered three miles north of Republic, burned 667 acres.  Meanwhile, several other fires briefly brought an impact to the general public.  Four miles south of Pullman, the Hatley Road Fire burned 50 acres and closed Highway 195 from milepost 15 to milepost 19.  This fire came close to several homes. Another fire in Davenport led to the Lincoln County Fair briefly being evacuated.  This fire also closed Highway 2.  Another fire occurred six miles southwest of Davenport, burning 600 acres.  The fire knocked out power to Davenport, destroyed one barn, closed Highway 28, and sporadically closed Highway 2.

Strong winds behind a cold front resulted in significant blowing dust over portions of the Waterville Plateau.  The Department of Highways reported zero visibility on State Route 172 between mileposts 18 and 22 near Mansfield, resulting in a road closure.

A thunderstorm containing very heavy rain led to a significant road washout just south of Tonasket on McLaughlin Canyon and Hardy Roads.  McLaughlin Canyon Road was washed out 10 feet deep and 15 feet across.  Hail to the size of dimes was also observed during the thunderstorm.  Some trails on a residence property was also washed out.

An early season pacific storm brought heavy snow to the Northeast Washington Mountains.  Bunchgrass Meadow and Quartz Peak SNOTEL's estimated 10 to 11 inches of snow.  The ski areas also were hard hit with 49 Degrees Ski Resort picking up 15 inches of new snow.

An Arctic cold front combined with Pacific moisture to produce widespread heavy snowfall and very cold temperatures. Snow and ice created dangerous driving conditions which led to numerous accidents.  Two fatalities occurred near Tieton on November 22.  Snowfall amounts in inches included Thorp (6), 5 miles north northwest of Ellensburg (5.5), 5 miles north northeast of Yakima (5), Toppenish (7.5), Yakima (4), Kennewick (8.5), Richland (4), 8 miles west northwest Connell (4.5), 2 miles south southeast of Richland (7), 3 miles north northwest of Waitsburg (5), 6 miles east of Dayton (4), Touchet SnoTel (8), Trout Lake (9), 7 miles north northwest of Easton (13), 2 miles north northwest of Goldendale (7), and Bickleton (9).||After the snow ended, clearing skies led to subzero temperatures in many areas including 12 below zero at Bickleton which was the coldest ever for November at Bickleton. Other subzero readings were at Ellensburg (-9), Hanford (-8), Pasco (-7), Selah (-6), Yakima (-10), Walla Walla (-2), Cle Elum (-5), Dayton (-8), Glenwood (-7), Goldendale (-3), Sagemoor (-11), Wapato (-7), Whitman Mission (-11) and Ski Bluewood (-10).
A weak EF0 tornado touched down in a field behind a home.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weak EF0 tornado touched down in a field behind a home with two eyewitnesses. Path length was 110 yards, width 5 yards. Damage limited to branches of up to 3 inches in diameter ripped off several  trees  and some deck plastic.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific frontal system brought strong winds to the southern Washington coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds were reported at Cape Disappointment with sustained winds of 40 kts and gusts to 59 kts.

Snowfall amounts in inches included Thorp (6), 5 miles north northwest of Ellensburg (5.5), 5 miles north northeast of Yakima (5), Toppenish (7.5), Yakima (4), Kennewick (8.5), Richland (4), 8 miles west northwest Connell (4.5), 2 miles south southeast of Richland (7), 3 miles north northwest of Waitsburg (5), 6 miles east of Dayton (4), Touchet SnoTel (8), Trout Lake (9), 7 miles north northwest of Easton (13), 2 miles north northwest of Goldendale (7), and Bickleton (9).||After the snow ended, clearing skies led to subzero temperatures in many areas including 12 below zero at Bickleton which was the coldest ever for November at Bickleton. Other subzero readings were at Ellensburg (-9), Hanford (-8), Pasco (-7), Selah (-6), Yakima (-10), Walla Walla (-2), Cle Elum (-5), Dayton (-8), Glenwood (-7), Goldendale (-3), Sagemoor (-11), Wapato (-7), Whitman Mission (-11) and Ski Bluewood (-10).

CZ_NAME: EAST SLOPES OF THE WASHINGTON CASCADES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture streaming northeast across the area ahead of an upper level trough produced heavy snow.  Around 40 vehicular accidents were reported in Kittitas County. Snowfall amounts in inches included Trout Lake (7), 7 miles north northwest of Easton (8), Glenwood (12), 4 miles west southwest of Roslyn (7), 4 miles southeast of BZ Corner (9.3), Touchet Mountain SnoTel (11), and Ski Bluewood (15).

Moisture streaming northeast across the area ahead of an upper level trough produced heavy snow.  Around 40 vehicular accidents were reported in Kittitas County. Snowfall amounts in inches included Trout Lake (7), 7 miles north northwest of Easton (8), Glenwood (12), 4 miles west southwest of Roslyn (7), 4 miles southeast of BZ Corner (9.3), Touchet Mountain SnoTel (11), and Ski Bluewood (15).

The Swakane Fire, centered nine miles north of Wenatchee, was a fire of unknown cause that burned 19,291 acres.  The fire briefly closed a section of Highway 97A.  One barn was destroyed by the fire, with numerous other structures threatened.

Slow moving thunderstorms containing very heavy rainfall led to flash flooding across portions of Central and Eastern Washington.  The west side of Wenatchee was hardest hit as a very slow moving thunderstorm brought 0.85 inches of rain in 45 minutes.  Road washouts were reported on Number 1 and Number 2 Canyon Roads.  Manhole covers were blown out in Downtown Wenatchee with standing water on some roads.  Four miles south of Wenatchee on Squillchuck Road, the heavy rain resulted in a mudslide.  Flash flooding resulted in water threatening homes, with several reports of house flooding.  Mud inundated indoor tennis courts at an athletic club. Another thunderstorm brought heavy rain just north of Entiat, sending a mudslide onto Highway 97A between mileposts 220 and 222.  The mud was up to six inches deep with two cars towed from the mud.  The road was closed during the night and opened the next day.  Another slow moving thunderstorm in Lincoln County resulted in road washouts along Highway 23 between Sprague and Harrington.

Windy conditions behind a dry cold front passage resulted in a dust storm over portions of the Moses Lake area and Upper Columbia Basin.  On Highway 261 ten miles south of Ritzville, the dust storm contributed to a two car accident closing the highway.  On the southeast side of Stratford in Grant County, county roads 18-NE and 20-NE were closed due to blowing dust and zero visibility.  Meanwhile in Cheney, a tree fell onto a power line, triggering an electric surge that ignited a home on fire. In Colfax, a strong wind gust blew a tree down onto a house.  In the Kettle Falls and Colville areas, gusty winds resulted in 1,600 customers losing power.  A 13 year old girl was injured by a falling branch which struck her in the head.  In Northeast Spokane 2,800 customers lost power.

The Rainbow Bridge Fire, centered 1.5 miles north of Stehekin, was a fire of unknown cause which burned about 3,710 acres.  The fire burned within a few hundred feet of the Stehekin School and the historic schoolhouse.  A couple homes were under mandatory evacuations.  The fire burned into the month of August.

Strong thunderstorms brought penny and nickel sized hail to near Waterville, Entiat, and Omak.  The strongest storm occurred on the Waterville Plateau where significant rain damage from flash flooding as well as nickel size hail fell.  Heavy rain with 2.25 inches in two hours was reported four miles northeast of Waterville which washed out a road.  The headwaters of Douglas Creek took out fences and parts of Road 11 NW.  The water was 10 feet deep in spots.  Some fields received a complete wheat loss. Meanwhile, heavy rain on the Nahahum fire north of Cashmere resulted in a shoulder being partially washed out on Nahahum Canyon Road between milepost four and five.
A spotter saw a small funnel cloud east of Enumclaw. It was brief and spotter could not see if it touched the ground due to the trees.
